includedSources.name: The Dark Side of the Boom

items.title: The UK's PPP GDP per capita now sits roughly where Mississippi's does
items.idea: UK's PPP GDP per capita is ~$56K — about the same as Mississippi. Middle-income Brits are 20% poorer than German peers and 9% poorer than French peers; low-income UK households sit £4,300 below their French equivalents. Since the UK and US should theoretically converge given similar institutions, the widening gap signals real institutional decay, not just bad luck.
items.angle: This is the kind of structural stagnation story that eventually shows up in currency and gilts — worth watching GBP and UK equities as a 'catch-down' trade if the pattern doesn't reverse.
items.tweetA: UK GDP per capita (PPP) now sits roughly where Mississippi's does. Not a meme — a Resolution Foundation data point. The scary part isn't the level, it's that a G7 economy with 'equivalent institutions' to the US was supposed to converge, not diverge. $GBP
items.tweetB: Everyone's debating whether Brexit or 2008 broke the UK more. Wrong question. Financialization was 12% of UK GDP vs 7% in the US — the UK bet the whole economy on the City of London, then the City's core industry crashed twice in 15 years. That's a single point of failure, not two separate ones.

charts.desc: Embedded charts: UK vs US price-adjusted income growth since 2008, income-per-capita vs US states, real wage divergence UK vs US since 2008.

items.title: New estimate: Brexit cut UK GDP by 6-8%, investment by 12-13%
items.idea: A 2025 academic study (Bloom et al.) using both macro synthetic-control and micro firm-level methods finds Brexit reduced UK GDP by 6-8% by end of 2025, cut investment 12-13%, employment 3-4%, productivity 3-4%. Exports are >30% of UK GDP vs just 11% for the US — meaning the UK was far more exposed to losing a trading bloc than the US currently is to any Trump tariff war.
items.angle: Direct read-through to tariff escalation risk: the US starts from a much lower trade-exposure base, so a full Brexit-style GDP hit is unlikely — but the multiplier math still applies at the margin.
items.tweetA: Brexit's bill, finally itemized: -6 to -8% GDP, -12 to -13% investment, -3 to -4% employment and productivity. Nine years later. If Trump's trade wars run the same math on 11% of GDP instead of 30%, the US version of this bill is smaller — but it's not zero.
items.tweetB: The UK was MORE globally integrated pre-Brexit than the US is pre-tariff-war (30% exports/GDP vs 11%). Everyone treats Brexit as a uniquely British self-own. It's actually the closest real-world natural experiment we have for what happens when a rich economy voluntarily shrinks its own market access.

charts.desc: Chart comparing UK GDP path vs synthetic-control counterfactual.

items.title: Britain's HS2 rail line: budget tripled to £100bn+; a bat tunnel needed 8,000 permits
items.idea: HS2's cost has tripled since 2009 to over £100 billion, making it the most expensive rail line in the world, with the London-Birmingham segment potentially not finished until 2040. Separately, Hinkley Point C nuclear plant's environmental review ran 31,401 pages and included a £700m 'fish disco' sound system to scare fish from intake pipes. A road tunnel project spent 32 years and £179m in planning before being scrapped this year.
items.angle: This is the strongest empirical case against NIMBY-driven veto-point regulation — worth citing whenever someone claims US permitting reform (NEPA, CEQA) doesn't matter for growth.
items.tweetA: Britain spent 32 years and £179 million PLANNING a road tunnel — cancelled this year without a single shovel in the ground. Meanwhile a nuclear plant needed a £700m sound system to scare off fish. This is what 'we take safety seriously' costs when there's no limiting principle on veto points.
items.tweetB: HS2's cost tripled to £100bn+ and the London-Birmingham leg might not open until 2040 — 31 years after approval. California's high-speed rail gets mocked for the same failure mode. It's not a US or UK problem, it's a rich-democracy-unlimited-veto-points problem, and it shows up in the bond math the same way everywhere.

items.title: Remove London and Britain would be poorer than any US state
items.idea: UK financial services are 12% of GDP vs ~7% in the US. John Burn-Murdoch calculates that stripping out London's output would cut UK living standards by 14% — enough to fall behind the poorest US state. The UK bet its whole economy on a single industry concentrated in a single city, and that industry has crashed twice (2008, the Euro Crisis aftermath).
items.angle: A single-industry, single-city economy is a fragile economy — worth remembering as the US runs an ever-growing share of S&P earnings growth through one theme (AI capex) and a handful of hyperscalers.
items.tweetA: Delete London from the UK's GDP and Britain falls below Mississippi. That's John Burn-Murdoch's math: -14% living standards. A G7 economy running on one industry in one city is the same fragility as a petrostate — just wearing a suit instead of a hard hat.
items.tweetB: The US is currently doing a milder version of the UK's finance-concentration mistake with AI capex — an ever-growing share of S&P earnings growth sourced from one theme, one cohort of hyperscalers. The UK spent 15 years learning what happens when the load-bearing industry wobbles. Worth remembering into this earnings season.

charts.desc: Electricity generation per capita chart; real wages UK vs US chart since 2008.

items.title: China's 'super-scale' model race: Qwen3.8 Max (2.4T params) vs Kimi K3 (2.8T params)
items.idea: Alibaba released a preview of Qwen3.8 Max at 2.4 trillion parameters days after Moonshot AI's Kimi K3 (2.8T params) roiled US tech stocks by rivaling Anthropic/OpenAI's top models. Both sit in a 'super-scale' (>2T param) class that barely existed a year ago. Alibaba positions Qwen3.8 Max as second only to Anthropic's Fable 5.
items.angle: Parameter count alone isn't a moat signal, but the cadence — two 2T+ frontier-track Chinese models inside a week — is the real story for anyone pricing in a multi-year US model lead.
items.tweetA: Two Chinese labs shipped 2T+ parameter frontier models in the same week (Kimi K3 at 2.8T, Qwen3.8 Max at 2.4T). A year ago 'super-scale' wasn't even a category. The US AI trade was priced on the assumption that this tier of model stays rare. It just got less rare, twice, in one week.
items.tweetB: Alibaba says Qwen3.8 Max ranks second only to Anthropic. Nobody should take a lab's own benchmark claim at face value — but the fact that a Chinese lab is even making that claim, in public, with a number, tells you where the confidence level has moved. $BABA

items.title: Alibaba open-sources its chip stack to challenge CUDA; Moonshot AI plots $30B IPO on $300M ARR
items.idea: Alibaba's chip unit T-Head is open-sourcing SAIL, the software stack for its Zhenwu AI chips, joining Huawei and Moore Threads in building an open alternative to Nvidia's CUDA lock-in. Separately, Moonshot AI (maker of Kimi K3) is seeking a Hong Kong IPO in ~6 months at a valuation over $30B, on just $300M of annualized recurring revenue — a 100x ARR multiple.
items.angle: The CUDA-moat attack is the more important story medium-term; the Moonshot IPO math ($30B on $300M ARR) is a useful sanity check for anyone pricing US AI startups at similar multiples.
items.tweetA: Nvidia's real moat was never the chip, it was CUDA — the software lock-in. Alibaba, Huawei, and Moore Threads are now all independently open-sourcing alternatives. Three separate well-funded actors attacking the same choke point simultaneously erodes faster than any single competitor could. $NVDA
items.tweetB: Moonshot AI: $300M ARR, seeking a $30B+ IPO valuation. That's 100x revenue for a three-year-old company. If that sounds crazy, check what US AI labs are being marked at on secondaries right now — this isn't a China-specific multiple, it's the going rate for 'frontier model lab' as an asset class globally.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: The AI trade's real risk isn't whether China can build a frontier model — it's who captures the margin
items.idea: Kimi K3 doesn't need to beat OpenAI or Anthropic outright — it only needs to prove frontier capability is getting cheaper and harder to monopolize. If customers get comparable results without paying premium US rates, the ROI on hundreds of billions in US AI infrastructure spend becomes harder to defend, even though aggregate AI demand keeps growing.
items.angle: This reframes the whole 'AI bubble' debate — the demand-side story is fine, the question was always margin capture, and China keeps answering it the same way: cheaper.
items.tweetA: The AI bear case was never 'nobody will use AI.' It's 'who gets paid for it.' Every time a cheaper Chinese model matches a premium US one, that's not a demand data point, it's a margin-capture data point — and margin capture is what's priced into $NVDA, $MSFT, $GOOGL multiples.
items.tweetB: Kimi K3 doesn't have to be the best model in the world. It just has to be good enough at half the API price to make a CFO ask why they're paying full freight to OpenAI. That question, asked enough times, is worth more to the AI trade's multiple than any single benchmark score.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Morgan Stanley: US capex growth hit 33% in 2025, forecast 23% (2026) and 26% (2027)
items.idea: Morgan Stanley's Andrew Sheets finds Russell 1000 capex growth averaged ~7%/year 2010-2025, then jumped to 33% in 2025, with 23% and 26% forecast for 2026-27. Global M&A volumes are up 64% YoY off historic lows. Sheets maps the cycle to 1997-98 and 2005-06, recommending owning volatility (gamma) over harvesting carry (theta) — equities over credit, since rising debt issuance to fund the capex boom will pressure spreads.
items.angle: A genuinely different framing than the AI-capex-bubble narrative: this treats the buildout as a broad, multi-sector capital cycle (AI + energy + Asia), not a narrow tech story, and translates it directly into an options-positioning call.
items.tweetA: Capex growth: 7%/year average for 15 years, then 33% in one year (2025), with 23% and 26% forecast for the next two. That's not 'the AI companies are spending a lot' — that's the biggest acceleration in corporate capital spending in over a decade, and it's showing up in credit spreads before it shows up in earnings.
items.tweetB: Morgan Stanley's read: this is 1997-98, not 2005-06. In '97-98 the winning trade was staying long risk while owning protection — gamma over theta. In '05-06 selling vol worked until it didn't. If Sheets is right about the analogue, the 'sell covered calls on your Mag7 position' crowd is playing the wrong decade's game.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: China's share of EU imports rose to 23% — yet STOXX 600 still beats CSI 300 by 5x this year
items.idea: Chinese exports into Europe are gaining share (23% of EU imports, up from 21% two years ago) across autos, medical tech, and chemicals — sectors Europe once considered its competitive moat. Yet STOXX 600 is up ~8% YTD vs CSI 300's ~1.5%, because European indices are now weighted toward services, semis equipment, and healthcare relatively insulated from Chinese competition.
items.angle: The index-vs-economy gap is the trade: Europe's industrial base is losing to China, but the benchmark holds far less of that industrial base than investors assume — auto/chemical exposure is what to avoid, not 'Europe' as a bloc.
items.tweetA: China's EU import share: 21% to 23% in two years, eating autos, medtech, and chemicals — Europe's old competitive moat. STOXX 600 is still beating the CSI 300 by 5x this year anyway. The index doesn't care that the industrial economy is losing, because the index isn't the industrial economy anymore.
items.tweetB: If you're short Europe because 'China is eating their lunch,' check what you're actually short. Autos and chemicals, yes. Semis equipment, healthcare, financials, telecom — largely insulated. The trade isn't long/short Europe, it's long/short the slice of the index still exposed to 1990s-style industrial competition.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (STOXX 600 vs CSI 300 comparison likely).

items.title: Ex-tech S&P 500 profits are projected to grow 14.3% this quarter — the broadening story nobody's pricing
items.idea: Deutsche Bank's Binky Chadha finds 'rest of S&P 500' (excluding tech/AI) profit growth is set to jump to 14.3% this quarter, adding 9.1 percentage points to total index growth, coinciding with ISM manufacturing back in expansion. Consensus expects 23.4% total S&P growth — above what ISM/PMI-implied models suggest (BofA's Savita Subramanian: ISM implies ~10%, services PMI implies ~20%).
items.angle: If ex-tech earnings actually deliver, that's the bullish scenario nobody's positioned for — the market has spent 4 years pricing 'narrow leadership,' and a genuine broadening would be a bigger regime change than another AI beat.
items.tweetA: Ex-AI, ex-tech S&P 500 earnings growth: 14.3% this quarter, adding 9 points to the index total. Everyone's watching whether Nvidia beats. The actual inflection point is whether the OTHER 493 companies keep growing at double digits — because that's the scenario nobody's hedged for.
items.tweetB: Consensus wants 23.4% S&P earnings growth this quarter. The ISM-implied number is 10%. Services PMI implies 20%. Even the more optimistic model falls short of consensus. Something has to give — either tech's contribution is more concentrated than the 'broadening' narrative admits, or consensus is simply too high.

charts.desc: Bloomberg earnings-estimate-by-sector chart; ISM-vs-S&P-earnings historical regression chart.

items.title: Apple is the best-performing Magnificent Seven stock this year — and only the 134th best in the S&P 500
items.idea: Trivariate's Adam Parker notes all Magnificent Seven stocks except Apple and Alphabet have underperformed the median S&P 500 stock this year, even though the 'breadth' narrative looks resolved on the surface — the torch has simply passed to semiconductors, what Bloomberg calls a 'rolling bubble' rather than a burst one.
items.angle: If AI infrastructure names (semis) report strong capex ROI in coming weeks, that's bad news for underweight-Mag7 active managers who thought they'd already dodged the concentration risk by rotating.
items.tweetA: Apple: best-performing Magnificent Seven stock in 2026. Also: 134th best-performing stock in the S&P 500. Sit with that gap — 'best of the mega-cap winners' now means 'middling, at best' against the broader market. The baton didn't get passed to breadth, it got passed to semis.
items.tweetB: Fund managers who went underweight the Magnificent Seven this year to 'de-risk' are about to find out they just rotated into a MORE concentrated bet, not a less concentrated one. If semis print strong capex ROI this earnings season, the 'rolling bubble' rolls right over their new positioning.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (Mag7 vs median stock performance chart).

items.title: NYC subway ridership still down a third from pre-pandemic — office construction hasn't slowed at all
items.idea: Torsten Slok's data shows weekday NYC subway ridership remains ~33% below pre-COVID levels, five-plus years on — evidence WFH is a permanent behavioral shift. Yet the skyline has added major new office towers since 2020 (One Vanderbilt, JPMorgan's 270 Park Ave, 10,000-worker capacity) even as office REITs have underperformed the rest of real estate specifically since ChatGPT's 2022 launch.
items.angle: The office REIT trade is a bet on whether a generation that never built in-person workplace habits will eventually re-adopt them — a generational bet dressed up as a real estate one.
items.tweetA: NYC subway ridership: still down a third from pre-COVID, five-plus years later. Meanwhile developers just finished 270 Park Ave (JPMorgan's new HQ, 10,000 desks) and One Vanderbilt. The market keeps building the office supply that the ridership data says nobody's commuting to fill.
items.tweetB: Office REIT underperformance didn't start with COVID — it accelerated specifically after ChatGPT launched in 2022. That's a weirder correlation than it looks: the WFH-tech-adoption curve and the AI-tech-adoption curve may be riding the same underlying comfort-with-remote-tools trend. Worth separating cause from correlation before betting the REIT bottom is in.

charts.desc: Subway ridership weekday-vs-weekend chart (Apollo/Slok); WFH Research paid-days-worked-from-home chart; office REIT relative performance chart.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Hedge funds net-sold US tech 6 of the last 8 weeks — largest cumulative unwind in Goldman's decade-plus dataset
items.idea: Goldman's prime brokerage data shows global long-short hedge funds lost 2.8% last week (1.5% from alpha alone), down 4.4% in July but still +13% YTD. Asia-focused funds had their worst week on record (-8%+) but remain +22% YTD. Information Technology was the most heavily net-sold US sector, a 1.7 standard-deviation move, split roughly evenly between long cuts and new shorts.
items.angle: This reads less like panic and more like risk-management discipline — funds are still up double digits YTD and cutting into strength-turned-weakness. Worth tracking whether selling exhausts before earnings season; Goldman's John Flood is already buying the drawdown.
items.tweetA: Hedge funds have net-sold US tech for 6 of the last 8 weeks — the biggest cumulative unwind in Goldman's 10+ year dataset, tied only with summer 2024. And they're still up 13% YTD. This isn't capitulation, it's profit-taking discipline dressed up as a selloff headline.
items.tweetB: Asia-focused long-short funds just had their worst week on Goldman's records — down more than 8% — and are still sitting on +22% YTD. Do that math: you can have a record-bad week and still be having a great year. Context is doing a lot of work the headline number skips.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (Goldman prime brokerage sector-flow chart).

items.title: US-Iran war ("Operation Epic Fury") hits day six-plus; Strait of Hormuz effectively shut
items.idea: The conflict that began February 28 has escalated into a sixth-plus consecutive day of US airstrikes against Iran, with the Strait of Hormuz effectively closed to shipping and Brent crude pushing back above $90 for the first time in six weeks. Crypto has decoupled from the war as a direct risk-off trigger, but not from the oil-and-Fed transmission channel.
items.angle: The oil move is the whole ballgame for the Fed's next print — six months of disinflation data gets stress-tested against a live energy shock in real time.
items.tweetA: Day six-plus of US airstrikes on Iran. Hormuz effectively closed. Brent back above $90 for the first time in six weeks. The market's been trading a Fed pivot on cooling inflation data — all collected BEFORE this escalation. July's print is about to test whether that data holds up against a live oil shock.
items.tweetB: Crypto stopped reacting to Iran headlines directly months ago — but it hasn't stopped reacting to what Iran does to oil, and what oil does to the Fed. That's the transmission mechanism now: geopolitics to oil to rates to risk assets, with crypto just riding the last leg at a 0.96 correlation to equities.

items.title: CPI fell 0.4% MoM — biggest drop since April 2020 — and July hike odds collapsed to 10-13%
items.idea: This week's soft CPI (-0.4% MoM) and PPI (-0.3% MoM) prints, plus jobless claims falling to 208,000, collapsed July Fed hike odds from above 40% to just 10-13%. But the data captures June, when Iran tensions had briefly eased — the ongoing strikes and shut Hormuz Strait mean July's print carries real reversal risk on the energy side.
items.angle: A 'trust but verify' macro setup — the dovish surprise that rallied risk assets mid-week is already stale relative to the live oil shock; September hike odds are the real tell, not July's already-priced reprieve.
items.tweetA: CPI's biggest monthly drop since April 2020, hike odds collapsed to ~10% from 40%+ — and it's all data from BEFORE the current Iran escalation restarted. Markets rallied on a snapshot that's already out of date. The real test is whether July's print, taken during a live oil shock, holds the same disinflation story.
items.tweetB: Jobless claims at 208K, CPI -0.4%, hike odds near zero — this is what a Fed pivot narrative looks like right before an energy shock ruins it. 2022's 'transitory' call died the same way: cooling data followed by a supply shock nobody priced into the forecast.

items.title: Bitcoin's correlation to equities just hit a record 0.96 — crypto now trades the AI narrative
items.idea: Bitcoin is reportedly now less volatile than South Korean stocks, and its correlation to equities has hit a record 0.96. The AGI Summit SF 2026 (OpenAI, Anthropic, Nvidia) is being framed as the catalyst that moves crypto more than the Fed or the CLARITY Act — meaning the semiconductor tape, not on-chain data, is now the leading indicator for BTC direction.
items.angle: This undercuts the 'crypto as macro hedge / uncorrelated asset' thesis in the near term — if you're trading BTC, watch NVDA and hyperscaler capex commentary, not funding rates or CLARITY Act headlines.
items.tweetA: Bitcoin is now LESS volatile than South Korean stocks, and its correlation to equities just hit a record 0.96. The 'crypto is an uncorrelated store of value' pitch is dead for now — BTC is trading as a leveraged AI-sentiment proxy. Watch chip earnings, not halving cycles.
items.tweetB: If you want to know where Bitcoin goes next week, don't check the CLARITY Act calendar or the Fed dot plot — check whether the AGI Summit reinforces or deepens AI-capex-ROI doubt. Crypto-native catalysts have stopped being the crypto-native catalysts.

items.title: Solana hits record $3.47B in tokenized assets while price sits at make-or-break support
items.idea: Solana's fundamentals are diverging sharply from price: record $3.47B in tokenized real-world assets, $900M in RWA inflows over 30 days, the largest RWA holder count of any chain (300,130), and fresh institutional validation as Japan's SBI Holdings acquired a majority stake in Singapore's Coinhako, deepening SBI's existing Solana Foundation and Ondo partnerships. Yet SOL trades at $75.20, testing critical $74-75 support with the weakest ETF flows ($1.7M) of any major crypto asset.
items.angle: A classic build-vs-price divergence — the fundamental case is arguably the strongest of any L1 right now, but flows haven't caught up, setting up either a big re-rate or a value trap depending on whether institutional demand actually follows the infrastructure.
items.tweetA: Solana: record $3.47B in tokenized real-world assets, $900M in RWA inflows in 30 days, and Japan's SBI just took a stake in a Solana-aligned exchange. The price? Sitting at make-or-break support with the weakest ETF flows of any major token. Fundamentals and flows have never been this far apart on one chain.
items.tweetB: Everyone's watching Bitcoin's ETF flows. The more interesting divergence is Solana's: the infrastructure build-out is objectively the strongest in crypto right now, and the market is pricing it like nothing's happening. That gap closes one of two ways — flows catch up, or the fundamentals were never the thing driving price.

items.title: BTC ETF flows flip: +$368M in 3 days while ETH ETFs lose $28M, ending their streak
items.idea: Institutional flow preference just rotated back toward Bitcoin — a broad-based $368M three-day inflow streak across all three major BTC ETF issuers (IBIT, FBTC, BITB) — while Ethereum's ETF inflow streak broke with a $28M outflow, even though ETH remains the only major crypto asset with a positive YTD (~+40%) while BTC is down 26.55% YTD.
items.angle: Two different time horizons telling two different stories — flows say rotate to BTC now, YTD performance says ETH's structural leadership is intact. The trade is holding both, not picking one.
items.tweetA: BTC ETF flows: +$368M across three issuers in three days. ETH ETF flows: -$28M, streak broken. But ETH is still up ~40% YTD while BTC is down 26%. Flows and performance are pointing in opposite directions on the same asset class this week — that's not noise, that's two different investor bases making two different bets.
items.tweetB: The flow inversion toward BTC isn't really bullish news, it's a tell about risk appetite. Institutions rotate to Bitcoin when they want the 'safer' crypto beta, not more of it. A broadening BTC ETF bid while ETH bleeds flows is defensive positioning inside the crypto sleeve, not a fresh bullish crypto allocation.

items.title: Every crypto exchange that IPO'd is down 70-90% from its debut: Gemini -89%, BitGo -77%, Bullish -71%
items.idea: The recent wave of crypto-exchange public listings has been a wipeout: Gemini is down 89% from its IPO, BitGo down 77%, Bullish down 71%. This comes even as underlying crypto adoption metrics (stablecoin volume, wallet users, institutional ETF infrastructure) continue to grow.
items.angle: This is the clearest evidence yet that 'crypto adoption is growing' and 'crypto-adjacent equities are investable' are two completely separate theses — the public listings priced in growth that didn't show up in the multiple.
items.tweetA: Gemini -89%, BitGo -77%, Bullish -71% from their IPOs. Meanwhile Circle just issued $500M of USDC on Solana and Bitget crossed 100M wallet users. The lesson: crypto adoption is real and crypto-exchange IPO pricing was not. Don't confuse the network growing with the ticker being a good trade.
items.tweetB: Three crypto exchanges went public within roughly the same window and all three are down 70%+. That's not three unrelated bad trades, that's a cohort effect — the entire IPO pricing model for crypto infrastructure companies was wrong at the same moment, which usually means underwriters were pricing off each other's comps, not fundamentals.

items.title: The AI selloff isn't about China, cost competition, or Iran — it's a leveraged, crowded-trade unwind
items.idea: Citrini argues the popular narratives for the recent AI-stock selloff (open-source competition, Kimi K3, cost compression, even the Iran conflict) don't actually explain the magnitude — industries relatively insulated from an energy shock have underperformed too. The real driver: a 'blistering, leverage-fueled breakout' of the most crowded AI and thematic trades is unwinding with the same force it built up with, independent of any single fundamental catalyst.
items.angle: If this framing is right, the selloff's duration depends on positioning/leverage metrics (see item #13's hedge-fund data), not on the next earnings print or the next Chinese model release — which changes what indicator to actually watch.
items.tweetA: The AI selloff has a fundamental-sounding narrative attached to every leg down — Kimi K3, cost competition, Iran. Citrini's point: none of it actually explains the magnitude, because industries insulated from an oil shock sold off too. The real story is leverage unwinding at the same velocity it built. Read the positioning data, not the headlines.
items.tweetB: 'The unwind generally mirrors the vigor of the ascent' is the single best sentence for this tape. Nobody needed a reason for AI stocks to go up 40% in a straight line. Nobody needs a fundamental reason for them to give it back the same way. Leverage doesn't need a narrative to unwind, it just needs a reason to start.

items.title: Only 2-3 daily transits through Hormuz, zero VLCC traffic — Iran still hitting tankers despite US strikes
items.idea: MacroEdge reports the Strait of Hormuz is down to just 2-3 daily transits with no VLCC (very large crude carrier) traffic in several days — and Iran continues disabling tankers and cargo ships with anti-ship missiles inside the Strait despite ongoing US strikes, including another tanker hit within the hour of the note. Brent is above $90, WTI near $85 — levels the author calls 'much more sustainable' than a $110-120 range, but still tight given the shutdown.
items.angle: The efficacy question here is the trade — if US strikes aren't stopping Iran's tanker-disabling capability, oil's risk premium has more room to run than the current $90 Brent level suggests, regardless of how the ceasefire-vs-escalation coin flip resolves.
items.tweetA: 2-3 tanker transits a day through Hormuz. Zero VLCC traffic in days. And Iran just disabled another tanker with an anti-ship missile — DURING active US airstrikes. If six-plus days of strikes haven't stopped Iran's ability to hit ships in the Strait, the market pricing $90 Brent as 'the escalation premium' might be underpricing what 'no de-escalation' actually looks like.
items.tweetB: Everyone's framing this as ceasefire-vs-escalation, 50/50. There's a third option nobody's pricing: a stalemate where US strikes continue, Iran keeps disabling ships anyway, and Hormuz just stays effectively shut for months. That breaks the 'oil settles back down once things calm' consensus, because nothing has to calm down — it just has to stay exactly this bad. $USO $XOM $CVX

items.title: NVDA's chart shows a wedge breakdown target near 120 — or an unlikely upside target near 250
items.idea: MacroEdge's technical read shows the Magnificent Seven basket (MAGS) with a significant negative divergence on both weekly and monthly timeframes — a pattern the author says usually signals longer-term trend reversals, not short-term noise. A wedge breakdown on NVDA specifically would target roughly 120, versus an upside pattern target near 250 the author calls unlikely for the second half absent major government intervention in the AI space.
items.angle: Compounding divergences on the monthly timeframe is the detail that matters — weekly divergences resolve in weeks, monthly ones historically take quarters, arguing for treating this as a multi-month risk, not a one-week dip to buy.
items.tweetA: NVDA's technical setup: a wedge breakdown targets ~120. The upside case targets ~250, but the author calls it unlikely without 'major government intervention.' Read that twice — the bull case for the most important stock in the market now explicitly requires policy support, not just earnings execution.
items.tweetB: Weekly negative divergences happen constantly and mostly mean nothing. Monthly ones are rarer and historically precede actual trend reversals. MAGS is showing negative divergence on BOTH timeframes right now. That's the technical version of 'this time might actually be different' — worth more attention than the headline price action suggests. $NVDA

charts.desc: MAGS weekly negative-divergence chart; NVDA wedge pattern chart with 120/250 target levels marked.

items.title: Auto repossessions running at 2009-crisis levels — while auto sales stay elevated on the wealth effect
items.idea: MacroEdge flags that automotive loan delinquency data and repo rates are sitting at levels last seen in 2009, even as light vehicle/truck sales remain elevated, propped up by the wealth effect from rising asset prices. The author notes the metric isn't adjusted for population or cars-on-the-road, but still calls it a genuine mixed signal amid broadly poor federal data quality.
items.angle: A classic K-shaped consumer tell — asset-holders keep buying cars on wealth-effect confidence while the credit-stressed cohort defaults at crisis-era rates, and the sales headline masks the divergence.
items.tweetA: Auto repo rates: back to 2009 levels. Auto sales: still elevated. Those two facts shouldn't coexist in a healthy consumer economy — they coexist here because the wealth effect from asset gains props up sales for people who own stocks, while people who don't are defaulting at financial-crisis rates. The 'resilient consumer' headline is really two different consumers.
items.tweetB: Every consumer-health data point right now needs the same disclaimer MacroEdge attaches to this one: federal survey-based data quality is degrading across the board. Nobody's fully sure how much of the 'resilient consumer' story is real versus a measurement artifact. That uncertainty is itself a risk factor the market isn't pricing.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Goldman: core PCE ran just 0.18% MoM in June — and Hatzius thinks even that overstates real inflation
items.idea: Goldman estimates core PCE rose a benign 0.18% MoM in June, with core CPI slightly negative and trimmed-mean PCE even softer. Jan Hatzius argues official core PCE overstates underlying inflation due to temporary tariff effects, energy pass-through, and service-category measurement distortions. Wage growth has cooled to 3.4%. Goldman remains below market pricing for Fed policy — but flags the real tail risk as a collision between persistently high energy prices and a deeper AI equity correction, hitting consumers via purchasing power AND the wealth effect simultaneously.
items.angle: The 'collision' risk Hatzius names is the connective tissue between three other items in this scan (Hormuz oil risk, HF tech selling, auto-delinquency wealth-effect divergence) — the single scenario that turns four separate soft stories into one hard one.
items.tweetA: Goldman's own inflation read is even softer than the official core PCE print — Hatzius thinks the government number is overstating inflation, not understating it. That's Goldman explicitly betting the market is more hawkish than the data justifies. Below-market Fed pricing from the desk everyone watches for hawkish calls is itself a signal.
items.tweetB: The scariest sentence in this whole Sunday news cycle isn't about oil or AI stocks separately — it's Hatzius naming the combo: sustained high oil PLUS a deeper AI correction hits consumers twice, through their gas bill and their portfolio, at the same time. Every other soft-landing data point this week assumes that combo doesn't happen.

items.title: The 2026 global growth reacceleration is running out of road as central banks pivot back to hikes
items.idea: Callum Thomas argues the global growth reacceleration seen through 2026 was driven by successive waves of monetary easing, and those tailwinds are now turning as central banks pivot back toward rate hikes given lingering inflation risk — setting up a 'distinctly different macro picture' heading into 2027. He also flags a persistent relative-value extreme favoring Global/Small/Value over US/Large/Growth that hasn't yet resolved.
items.angle: This dovetails with other synchronized-hiking-pivot data points this week — a global regime call, not a Fed-only story, to watch into year-end.
items.tweetA: 2026's growth reacceleration ran on rate cuts. Those cuts are now reversing into hikes globally, not just at the Fed. The setup for 2027 is a completely different macro regime than what drove this year's rally — and most portfolios are still positioned for the regime that's ending, not the one that's starting.
items.tweetB: Global/Small/Value has been 'about to turn' against US/Large/Growth for what feels like forever, and Topdown Charts is flagging it again: relative value extremes without a catalyst. The lesson from every prior cycle is that this rotation doesn't trade on valuation alone, it needs the growth/rate regime to actually flip first — which is exactly what's happening now.

charts.desc: Hero chart: 'Tailwinds Turning' — global monetary policy stance vs growth reacceleration.

items.title: Equal-weighted space stock index just had a -55% correction and is testing key technical support
items.idea: Topdown Charts is establishing new coverage on space stocks, noting the equal-weighted space index has undergone a major -55% correction and is now testing a key support level — flagged as a newly interesting, under-covered theme.
items.angle: A -55% drawdown in a thematic index that barely existed as an investable category five years ago is a data point worth filing away — space/defense-adjacent names have gone from momentum darlings to washout candidates with almost no mainstream coverage of the move.
items.tweetA: Space stocks: -55% correction, equal-weighted, and almost nobody's talking about it. Compare that to the airtime AI infrastructure drawdowns get at a fraction of the percentage decline. The theme that was 'the next AI' 18 months ago just had a crash nobody covered.
items.tweetB: New coverage initiations after a -55% drawdown are usually either early or a value trap. The tell will be whether this support level holds on the first retest — space is small enough cap that a real washout here could be one of the cleaner reversion setups in the market, if the sector actually has a business model underneath the theme.

charts.desc: Equal-weighted space stock index chart with support level marked.

items.title: 70-75% cash for a month straight — most megacaps 30-35% off highs with weak technicals
items.idea: Six Sigma Capital has held 70-75% cash for roughly a month given a lack of quality setups, noting most leading megacap names (semis, space, AI supply chain, robotics) are 30-35% off their highs with only a handful above their 50-day moving average. Software and healthcare are flagged as the more actionable, stronger sectors right now.
items.angle: A high-conviction, high-cash stance from someone whose job is to trade momentum is itself a data point — when the momentum crowd goes to cash, that's often a better regime read than any single indicator.
items.tweetA: A momentum trader sitting in 70-75% cash for a month, watching semis, space, AI supply chain, and robotics all trade 30-35% off highs with almost none above their 50-day. That's not caution, that's someone whose entire strategy depends on trends existing telling you there currently aren't any worth trading.
items.tweetB: Notice what's NOT on the beaten-down list: software and healthcare. While AI hardware and 'AI adjacent' momentum names get crushed 30%+, software (the actual application layer of AI) is holding up better. That's either rotation working exactly as textbook says it should, or a warning that hardware capex concerns are starting to bite before software monetization concerns do.

charts.desc: StockCharts technical-strength ranking table of leading megacaps.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: 'People will believe the most preposterous stories, as long as the price keeps going up' — a 400-year-old line for this week's tape
items.idea: The Boom Daily quotes market historian Bob Hoye, a 50-year friend of the newsletter's author, on the timelessness of speculative narrative: the same line applies equally to Tulipmania in 1630s Holland, 1929, the dot-com bubble, and today's markets. It's used as a framing device for the current 'runaway markets.'
items.angle: Not a data point, a pattern-recognition tool — useful shorthand for any thread about narrative-chasing in AI stocks or crypto without rebuilding the historical case from scratch each time.
items.tweetA: 'People will believe the most preposterous stories, as long as the price keeps going up.' Written about Tulipmania. Applies word for word to Kimi K3 selloffs, AI capex debates, and crypto flow inversions. The stories change every cycle. The mechanism by which people believe them hasn't changed since 1630s Holland.
items.tweetB: The most useful thing about a 400-year-old market quote isn't that it's clever, it's that it's a falsifiable test: if the narrative only holds while the price is rising, it's not a thesis, it's a rationalization. Apply that filter to every AI or crypto argument you're hearing this week and see what survives.

items.title: Stripe and Advent's $60.50/share bid for PayPal — valuing it above $53B — is still live
items.idea: A joint Stripe/Advent bid for PayPal at $60.50/share, valuing the company above $53 billion, remains an active situation. PayPal's stablecoin, PYUSD, has a market cap of $2.8 billion.
items.angle: A private-equity-plus-strategic bid for a payments incumbent is a fintech consolidation signal worth tracking alongside the PYUSD numbers — Stripe buying distribution and stablecoin float in one deal reshapes the issuer league table overnight if it closes.
items.tweetA: Stripe + Advent, $60.50/share, $53B+ for PayPal. If that closes, Stripe doesn't just buy a checkout button, it buys PYUSD's $2.8B in stablecoin float and PayPal's entire merchant network in one transaction. That's a bigger stablecoin-market-share move than anything happening on-chain this week. $PYPL
items.tweetB: Everyone's tracking USDC and USDT issuance as the stablecoin story. The actual stablecoin land-grab might be happening through M&A, not new token launches — Stripe acquiring PYUSD's distribution via a PayPal buyout is a faster path to share than out-competing Tether organically.
